title: Overnight oats
subtitle: Oats soaked overnight in milk with yoghurt and fruit.
excerpt: Porridge oats soaked overnight in milk and yoghurt with a spoonful of honey. Ready in the morning without any cooking. Topped with fresh fruit or granola. Serves two and takes five minutes to prepare.
sourceNotes: Cold-soaked oats were popularised by Swiss physician Maximilian Bircher-Benner around 1900 as the original Bircher Muesli. The overnight oats version, distinguishable from Bircher by the absence of grated apple and the use of milk rather than apple juice as the soaking liquid, became widely popular in Britain from around 2012 through health-food culture and meal-prep trends.

recipe.batchNotes: Make four to five portions at once and keep in the fridge for up to four days.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Made the night before by design. Can soak for up to 48 hours.

content.text: The ratio of oats to liquid is the only thing that matters here. Too little liquid and the oats are dry and chalky in the morning. Too much and they are soupy. The right ratio is 1 part oats to 1.5 parts liquid by volume — or weigh it as 100 g oats to 150 ml liquid per serving.
The toppings go on in the morning, not the night before. Fresh fruit added at night bleeds colour into the oats, and anything crunchy softens overnight.
A bowl or jar with a lid. No cooking equipment needed.

Combine {{porridge-oats}} of oats, {{whole-milk}} of milk, {{plain-yoghurt}} of yoghurt, {{honey}} of honey, and {{vanilla-extract}} of vanilla in a bowl or jar. Add the chia seeds if using. Stir well. Cover and refrigerate overnight, or for at least 6 hours.
Stir the oats. If they have thickened too much, add a splash of cold milk. Top with blueberries and banana, or whatever fruit is available. Serve cold directly from the jar.

items.symptom: Oats are too thick in the morning
items.cause: The oats absorbed more liquid than expected, or chia seeds were used.
items.fix: Stir in a splash of cold milk until the consistency you want. The oats will also loosen slightly as you eat them.

items.symptom: Oats are chalky and not fully softened
items.cause: Not enough soaking time, or too little liquid.
items.fix: Leave for at least 8 hours. Use the full 200 ml of liquid per 100 g of oats.

items.cause: Not enough honey or vanilla, or the milk is low-fat.
items.fix: Add more honey. Whole milk produces a noticeably creamier result than skimmed.

items.symptom: Fruit has made the oats pink or grey
items.cause: Fresh berries were added the night before.
items.fix: Always add fresh fruit in the morning. Frozen berries can be stirred in at night as they release less colour slowly.

content.text: Overnight oats are a simplification of Bircher muesli, itself a nineteenth-century Swiss invention. The modern format, plainer and made with milk rather than apple juice, became a specific internet phenomenon in the early 2010s alongside the meal-prep movement. It solved a real problem: a breakfast that required no morning effort and could be made five at a time on Sunday evening. The format has not changed much because it does not need to.
